title: Thermodynamic properties of chemically disordered compounds via AI-driven estimation of partition function with the PULSE method
authors: Baptiste Bernard, Luca Messina, Eiji Kawasaki, Emeric Bourasseau
abstract: In this article, we present an improved version of the PULSE method (Partition function Unsupervised Learning Sampling and Evaluation) for estimating the thermodynamic properties of chemically disordered compounds. The aim is to reduce the computational cost of Monte Carlo approaches for this type of material and to demonstrate that this generative tool can estimate thermodynamic properties by sampling and estimating the partition function of the system. To validate this innovative approach, we use the 2D Ising model as a benchmark. We demonstrate that our method accurately reproduces average properties with high precision and efficiency compared to traditional Monte Carlo sampling methods. Our results highlight the efficiency and adaptability of the PULSE method, making it a valuable tool for studying materials for which conventional methods are too inefficient to compute properties affected by chemical disorder at low cost.
summary: This paper introduces an improved AI-driven method called PULSE to estimate the partition function and thermodynamic properties of chemically disordered materials. By using an inverse Variational Autoencoder, the method achieves high precision and efficiency, particularly in high-temperature regimes, while scaling effectively with system size.
main_problem: Estimating the thermodynamic properties and partition functions of chemically disordered compounds is computationally expensive due to the massive configuration space required for traditional Monte Carlo convergence.
main_result: The improved PULSE method accurately and efficiently estimates partition functions and thermodynamic properties, outperforming Wolff and Wang-Landau algorithms in the high-temperature disordered regime and demonstrating excellent scalability.
method: An improved version of the PULSE method using an 'inverse' Variational Autoencoder (VAE) architecture and unsupervised learning to optimize a distribution that minimizes Kullback-Leibler divergence.
model_or_system: The method is validated using the 2D Ising model on a square lattice, serving as a proxy for complex chemically disordered materials like high-entropy alloys and MOX fuels.
key_observables: Partition function (Z), average energy, specific heat (Cv), magnetic susceptibility, and magnetization.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), inverse temperature (beta), system size (N), and number of samples (m).
main_assumptions: The observable property must be strictly positive for the logarithmic formulation; high-energy configurations are assumed to be negligible at low temperatures.
figures_summary: The figures illustrate the PULSE architecture, compare PULSE estimates to exact solutions for the partition function and magnetization, show the reconstruction of thermodynamic properties, and demonstrate scalability and error comparisons against Wolff and Wang-Landau algorithms.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific problem of chemical disorder, presents the mathematical framework of the improved PULSE method, validates the approach using the 2D Ising model, compares performance against traditional Monte Carlo methods, and discusses scalability and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1 – Schematic view of the PULSE inverse VAE architecture.

Figure 2 – Canonical partition function of the 4 × 4 Ising system, with the exact value (obtained by including all possible spin configurations in the calculation) shown as a blue curve and the PULSE estimation as purple dots. The convergence criterion is set to a 1% error relative to the exact value.

Figure 3 represents the PULSE estimation of Z|M|, i.e., the partition function associated with the absolute value of the magnetization (Eq. (17)). Similarly to the previous quantity, PULSE reaches an error below 1% compared to the exact value, using between 200 and 2 500 samples per tem- perature, depending on the target temperature. This shows that PULSE consistently achieves an outstanding accuracy on the partition function associated with specific properties, using a relatively low and stable amount of configurations.

Figure 3 – Partition function of the absolute value of magnetization for the 4 × 4 Ising system, with the exact value (obtained by including all possible spin configurations in the calculation) shown as a blue curve and the PULSE estimation as purple dots. The convergence criterion is set to a 1% error relative to the exact value.

Figure 4 – Average values of (a) normalized magnetization, (b) squa- red magnetization, and (c) modified susceptibility obtained as recom- binations of partition functions sampled by PULSE. Purple dots are recombinations of partition functions according to equations (14), (17), and (18). The blue curve marks the exact value (obtained by including all possible spin configurations in the calculation).
